title: Housing downturn possible as demand softens and rates kick in, Cotality warns
full_text:
The tight supply conditions that have underpinned the national housing market in recent years are starting to ease, according to property data firm Cotality.
This, combined with a softening in demand, has created the conditions for a national property market downturn.
Cotality research director Tim Lawless said Sydney and Melbourne were already five months into the early phases of decline, while growth was slowing across the mid-sized capitals.
"Listings are picking up as demand softens, interest rates are rising, while affordability and serviceability pressures are biting," he said.
Cotality research shows that, historically, there have been several catalysts for housing declines, including global shocks, rising interest rates, and periods of credit tightening, as well as changes in fiscal policy and broader market factors such as affordability and sentiment.
Independent economist Saul Eslake agrees that some elements are in place for continued weakness in the housing market.
"It is quite plausible that the combination of higher interest rates and greater caution on the part of prospective buyers [prompted by apprehension about the economic consequences of the conflict in the Middle East] could see a downturn in property prices."
This, he said, comes despite "the ongoing mismatch between the 'underlying' demand for housing and the supply of it."
In other words, demand is softening sufficiently to overwhelm the existing lack of supply and the upward pressure that naturally puts on prices.
"[A lack of supply] has been a characteristic of Australian housing markets for most of the past 30 years — but it hasn't prevented occasional downturns in the effective [as distinct from the underlying] demand for housing from causing brief periods of price declines," Mr Eslake said.
Put another way, "it's the effective demand for housing — what people are willing and able to pay — interacting with the supply of housing, not the 'underlying' demand [determined by population growth and household formation rates] — that matters for movements in prices over short periods."
Capital city prices to decline
With capital city home values only 0.2 per cent higher in April, and fading, Tim Lawless, Cotality's Asia Pacific head of research, said there was a good chance the combined capitals index would move into decline over the coming months as further momentum leaves the market.
"This trend has been amplified by 75 basis points [0.75 percentage points] of rate hikes so far this year and the chance of another hike, or hikes, later in the year," he said.
"Importantly, the market was already slowing well before the hiking cycle commenced, highlighting the downside impact of waning confidence from late last year alongside rising inflation and worsening levels of housing affordability."
Sydney dwelling values fell 0.6 per cent in April and are now 1.0 per cent below their November 2025 peak.
Melbourne values declined 0.6 per cent over the month and sit 2.3 per cent below their March 2022 high.
More vendors are now coming to market.
New listings have increased, with 39,319 properties added over the four weeks to early May, which is 4.7 per cent above the five-year average.
Total listings remain below average levels, at 127,821 properties nationally, down 2.6 per cent from a year ago.
Cotality noted that listing levels are increasing as buyer demand softens rather than because of a surge in new stock coming onto the market.
The total stock of advertised properties remains low nationally, but is rising, and holding at above average levels in Sydney, Melbourne and Canberra.
Recent buyers at risk of 'negative equity'
Cotality's report also noted that "more recent buyers are likely to be exposed to the risk of negative equity as values fall, particularly those who entered the market with smaller deposits, including participants who took advantage of the Australian Government 5% Deposit Scheme."
Negative equity occurs when the outstanding balance of a loan (such as a mortgage or car loan) is higher than the current market value of the asset securing it.
"Recent buyers are arguably at more risk of seeing negative equity, given they have had less time to accrue value in their property or pay down the principal of their loan," Mr Lawless said.
"However, mortgage repayments are typically prioritised, with borrowers more likely to adjust spending elsewhere before missing a payment."
And Mr Eslake said history shows policymakers are attuned to the plight of those facing negative equity.
“Those periods of price declines have all been fairly brief because whenever they have occurred, governments have responded to fears that 'the sky will fall in' [if house prices are allowed to fall substantially] by doing things designed to stop house prices from falling, such as doubling or trebling first home owners' grants, and encouraging the RBA to cut interest rates,” he said.
"Yes, it's probable that some people would find themselves in a 'negative equity' position … but as long as those folks keep their jobs, and hence can afford to continue to service their mortgages, they'll be OK in the long run."
Ongoing strength in some markets
Pockets of strength remain within the national housing market.
Perth values rose 26.0 per cent over the past year compared to just 2.0 per cent in Melbourne, with the gap in annual growth rates across the capital cities widening to 24 percentage points.
"An expectation that labour markets will remain tight, as well as a history of stringent prudential standards, including the three-percentage point mortgage serviceability buffer, should help to keep mortgage arrears low as interest rates rise," Mr Lawless said.
Indeed, the combined capitals Home Value Index has risen 33.7 per cent over the past five years, with markets such as Perth, Brisbane and Adelaide recording growth of around 80 per cent to 90 per cent over the same period.
"Historically, housing downturns have been relatively short-lived, with all but three capital city downturns over the past 40 years lasting less than 12 months, although the length and magnitude have varied from city to city," he said.
And, according to auctioneer Dennis Nutt, investors continue looking for value in the market.
"There's always investors in the market waiting — waiting for deals," he said.
"We're in that type of market now.
"We have vendors who may have purchased four years ago at the height of the market, [who] now, for whatever circumstances, need to sell."
Reality check for aspiring first home buyers
While the property market is turning, academics say it does not necessarily open the door to improved housing affordability.
A professor of urban and regional planning at Sydney University, Nicole Gurran, told the ABC, "People who most need to get into home ownership are least able to get a loan when prices fall dramatically.
"It's really an economic shock that triggers a dramatic downturn in property prices."
In this environment, Professor Gurran argued, "People can't afford to borrow [to buy a home] because they don't have stable incomes."
And, she added, lower-income households still have competition at the bottom end of the rental market.
Australian Broadcasting Corporation
Document ABCNEW0020260507em570015q
summary: Cotality warns that the Australian housing market may face a downturn due to softening demand and rising interest rates. Sydney and Melbourne are already experiencing declines, with increased listings and potential risks of negative equity for recent buyers. Despite these challenges, some markets like Perth continue to show strength.

heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Housing market downturn possible as demand softens
Sydney and Melbourne experiencing early phases of decline
Recent buyers at risk of negative equity due to falling values
relevance: Understanding housing market trends and risks is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals involved in property financing and investment.
